title: Cat Pacing and Unable to Settle: Causes and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Is your cat pacing and unable to rest? Learn about potential causes, urgency levels, and why a video can help your veterinarian diagnose the issue.
content_html:
What is Pacing and Restlessness in Cats?

Pacing occurs when a cat walks continuously, often in a repetitive pattern or in circles, without being able to settle down or sleep. Unlike normal activity, this behavior looks frantic or persistent. It is usually a clinical sign that your cat is experiencing physical discomfort, hormonal imbalances, or significant neurological or emotional distress.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. While pacing may not always require an immediate midnight trip to the ER, it is a sign that your cat is uncomfortable and needs a medical evaluation soon. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours. However, if the pacing is accompanied by an inability to urinate, open-mouth breathing, or sudden dragging of the hind legs, you must seek emergency care immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

When you arrive at the vet, your cat may stop pacing due to the stress of the environment or the surge of adrenaline. Capturing a short video of the behavior at home allows your veterinarian to see the exact nature of the restlessness. A video can help the vet distinguish between neurological circling, pain-related pacing, or behavioral anxiety, leading to a much faster and more accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Could my cat be pacing because of pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats often pace when they are experiencing internal pain, such as abdominal discomfort or joint pain, because they simply cannot find a position that feels good.

faqs.question: Can hyperthyroidism cause restlessness?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. An overactive thyroid gland is a common cause of increased energy, pacing, and vocalization, especially in middle-aged to senior cats.

faqs.question: Is pacing a sign of cat dementia?
faqs.answer: In older cats, pacing and wandering—especially at night—are frequent symptoms of Feline Cognitive Dysfunction, which is similar to dementia in humans.
